Thanks Robert, I've watched countless videos of experts explaining curves and lofts in Freecad and you've gone and done it in ten mins.
Thanks again and keep up the excellent work 👍

author: @ravenmad9225
text: "There is nothing,absolutely nothing,half so much worth doing as simply messing about in boats".

author: @ZacharyYbarra-u1g

author: @SAILBOATJACARANDA
text: I wonder if its worth doing some kind of textured surface on the 3d print that would allow for a skin of fiberglass. Would make an interesting composite process.

author: @ch5139
text: ‘Wisdom of Ignorance’ sounds like every episode of ‘Last it the Summer Wine’…a 3D printed canoe would certainly impress Nora Batty. Best get Luke to film you stepping into it for the first time in your wellies😀

author: @DaveLaneNZ
text: Superb choice! You selected the Peterborough design! My uncle and cousin have each built these very boats in the old style wood & canvas style on a form (which my cousin has in his barn!). Peterboroughs are know to be quite slippery, but also a bit tippy if unloaded... to be stable, they need a bit of weight in them. Have fun!

author: @timmyssweet
text: Excellent, I had been thinking better ways to 3d-printing fuselages and larger objects.  Strength is the biggest problem, I think.  In fortunately, long strand CF core filament is not affordable.  I was thinking of running channels in 3d printed segments and running wire through them, so they can be tightened.  You would need a compressible filament for this.  … something with a high amount of CF, I think.

author: @lagunafishing
text:
Question is, instead of using PLA what filament floats?
Filaments like PETG and certain types of nylon can float, depending on their density and the design of the printed object. Additionally, using a hollow design or incorporating air pockets can help improve buoyancy.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: The material you  make a boat from doesn't have to float mate - they make boats from steel. A boat floats if the weight of water displaced is less than the weight of the boat. The original boat weighs about 60kg in wood and will be about 20kgs in PLA - it's actually going to be too light for the designed waterline and that might be an issue

author: @chrisgill5692
text: Hmmm, really interesting, especially the next stage of structure and skinning.  Printed plastic is not watertight, but printing thin plate sections (curved for structure and flat for the skin) and bonding together - ie laminating - might work?  Pretty arduous process and lots of plastic so maybe some sub-scale trials.  Really looking forward to the next stage and your approach!!!!

author: @danblankenship5744
text:
Nice workbench.
Print an oar first.

You don't want to be up a creek without one.

author: @johnwynne-qx6br
text: Good luck with the new project sounds like a great plan. I have quite recently watched a video about a plastic narrow boat apparently first of it's kind and in production, its using same plastic as army and other sea boats that have been using it for years so obviously a good material. Not sure if it's a printable plastic? But sounds like the type to use if you can.  👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i will look into it mate - cheers

author: @woodygoeswondering
text: Great video as always! A few years ago when I was looking into building a boat I found a cad program called DELFTship. It’s relatively simple and fun to use and does most of the lofting or plank shaping for you. I’d certainly recommend giving it a try even if only for fun. In the meantime I look forward to seeing how you get on.

author: @thebytespider
text: Worth noting that the skin (surface) you created has effectively 0 width, so requires another step, thickening, to be able to export to stl. GREAT tutorial on how to use lofts to create complex shapes

replies.text: cheers mate and very true - thanks for taking the time to point that out - cheers

author: @delakota555
text:
There was a young man from Crewe
Who wanted to build a canoe
When he got to the river
He found with a shiver
He hadn't used waterproof glue.
🤣👍

replies.author: @John_B55
replies.text:
So he went to the pub for a think
And thought as he supped on his drink
The thing I've forgotten
Is to put paint on the bottom
And hopefully then I wont sink

author: @ExpressoMechanicTV
text: Another great piece of work!

author: @anthonynorris7736
text: Goodluck with your boat build 😊

author: @corwinwhitehorn7759
text: Proper Printing made one 3 weeks ago... there are thing to consider, as you already know... Layer adhesion being one of them

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: There has been a few made mate - including a couple of surf boards I saw that look cool - but to be honest I am really looking for an excuse to go through the curves and curved objects workbench - A canoe - sure that's nice but the workbench should help folks make a whole range of things if they want to

replies.author: @corwinwhitehorn7759
replies.text: I have to say I have absolutely nothing against curves 😁

replies.author: @chrisgill5692
replies.text: Ha, ha, just seen the Proper Printing video.  Crazy waste of filament, luckily sponsored by Prusament, but really interesting.  All the problems were predictable, but a fantastic effort and just love his homemade belt printer.

author: @Lifeonthepond
text:
Oh my...your one of my fav you tube creators..and that is the very model of cedar strip I built 35 years ago and  from that book. One of the first woodworking projects I ever  did. 

You might want to customize your center yoke to fit your shoulders for portages...of course yours will be much lighter than the 60 or so pounds the cedar and fiberglass version is.

 Because it will be so light you might want to consider a slight center keel for handling...the peterborough can be a little hard to steer in a stronger wind...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is so cool mate and thanks for the tips - I will bear them in mind - cheers

author: @grendel1960a
text: you might want to consider a feature to assist registration of the parts once you have split it into printable sections, registration pegs or similar to strengthen and align the joints

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Good point mate - when I get it made you coming for a paddle down the Stour with me?

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I do have a paddle board, so that could be an option.
